author: rasbt
text:
Polite users and LLMs are wasting millions of dollars!
Napkin math: 
> Say "Please" and "thank you" costs ~3 tokens per ChatGPT exchange. 
> At GPT-4o rates, that's ~$0.00003 each time. 
> Multiply by 800M chats/day and that's "wasting" ~$9.5M/year on politeness

text: True, but I think it's more of a proof-of-concept / study of synthetic data for pretraining rather than generating a production-ready model.

author: rasbt
text: Yeah, that's basically at the intersection of pretrained LLM and tool-use.

author: rasbt
text: Yeah, I think the main advantage is from the more refined nature of the synthetic data when it comes to response structure etc. Synthetic data can't really contain knowledge that raw data doesn't already include because the raw data was used to come up with the synthetic data in

author: rasbt
text: Yes. I think the main benefit is that it comes in a more structured or refined format compared to raw data. But the knowledge is the same as in the raw data (and may even be more hallucinated), considering that raw data was used to generate the synthetic data through a model.

author: rasbt
text: Yeah, I see it as some flavor of transfer learning (i.e., not starting from raw data). Synthetic data generated by a high-quality model (such as GPT-4o, which  has already undergone extensive refinement) may serve as a kind of  jumpstart to the model you are trying to train.

author: rasbt
text: Since the model can't really explicitly distinguish between synthetic and non-synthetic data during training, the best way would tackle the problem at the root: ensuring that the synthetic data-generating model does not produce hallucinated contents.

Oh and maybe the most important one: the conversion to Kelvin in sciences classes is much much easier 

Celsius to Kelvin
K = °C + 273.15

Fahrenheit to Kelvin
K = ((°F - 32) × 5/9) + 273.15

author: rasbt
text: PS: LoRA stands for *Lo*w-*R*ank *A*daptation. One of the unsolved mysteries of this paper is what the MoRA acronym stands for. Matrix-"o"-rank-adaptation?

author: rasbt
text:
Turns out that this rel. simple method works surprisingly well. It outperforms LoRA in continued pretraining. But trails LoRA by a bit in instruction finetuning and mathematical reasoning with small ranks. For me, the results are not compelling enough to swap LoRA for MoRA.
7/7

author: rasbt
text:
How do you apply this 256x256 matrix to the original 1024x1024 weight matrix? They define several non-parametric compression and decompression methods (the details are a bit out of scope for this post, but I tried to summarize it with PyTorch code in the figure).
6/7

author: rasbt
text:
For example, if the original weight layer has 4096 x 4096 = 16,777,216 parameters, LoRA with r=8 has 4096x8 + 8x4096 = 65,536 parameters. With MoRA, we can match the number of parameters via r=256, i.e., 256 x 256 = 65,536.
5/7

author: rasbt
text:
Here, they use a trainable square matrix applied to the original weights W instead of the matrices AB in LoRA. However, the number of trainable parameters in LoRA and MoRA can be the same. 
4/7

author: rasbt
text:
So, in the MoRA paper, the authors seek to develop a parameter-efficient finetuning method that can perform well for both instruction finetuning AND absorbing new knowledge in continued pretraining. 
3/7

author: rasbt
text: Why another LoRA alternative, now with high ranks? LoRA (I'd say that's by design) updates the original weights in a relatively limited well, which is great for tasks like instruction finetuning but relatively ineffective continued pretraining, which requires more capacity. 2/7

author: rasbt
text:
It's always exciting when a new paper with a LoRA-like method for efficient LLM finetuning comes out. In "MoRA: High-Rank Updating for Parameter-Efficient Finetuning (
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2405.12130)," the authors take a related yet opposite approach to low-rank adaptation.
1/7

author: rasbt
text: In SFT, the LLM still generates one token at a time, and the loss is also computed on a per-token basis, but it's accumulated for the generated tokens. It's 1 training task per Q&A pair.

author: rasbt
text:
What I mean is in pretraining if you have the text "Q: What is your favorite food? A: Ice cream"
You train on n-1 tasks as follows:
Q: -> What
Q: What -> is
Q: What is -> your
...
Q: What is your What is your favorite food? A: Ice -> cream

In SFT finetuning, you train only on 1

Alpha is essentially a scaling factor. If you have the lora weights lora_A and lora_B (for each layer), alpha determines how much of that gets added to the weights: 

scaling = alpha / r
weight += (lora_B @ lora_A) * scaling

author: rasbt
text:
What I was trying to say is: when have we had a computationally more efficient method producing much more impressive results than a previous method?

Efficient methods are usually used to get similar results in a much more efficient manner.

author: rasbt
text: Well yeah, for example, latent diffusion models are more efficient than the original diffusion models for sure. But to demonstrate the jump up from GANs, you had to pour in additional computational resources to make it impressive enough to get a technological jump, though.

text: Sure, but that’s why it’s a survey, not a research paper. There are usually 3 types of papers: Survey papers gather and organize references, review papers synthesize and discuss selected works in context and sometimes do a meta analysis, and research papers propose new methods.

author: rasbt

According to "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" (
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2307.03172) LLMs are good at retrieving information at the beginning of documents. 
They do less well in terms of retrieving information if its contained in the middle of a document.

2/5

author: rasbt
text:
We have seen a new wave of LLMs for longer contexts: 1) RMT, 2) Hyena LLM & 3) LongNet

There are several use-cases for such long LLMs but the elephant in the room is: How well do LLMs use these longer contexts? 
Turns out not so well if info is in the middle of the input.
1/5

author: rasbt
text:

But this maybe expected due to the large number of parameters when finetuning the whole LLM.

2/3

author: rasbt
text:
Common ways to finetune LLMs are to 
a) update the output layers vs 
b) update more (or all) layers. 

Usually, the more parameters we update, the better for target-task performance (wrote about here: 
https://
magazine.sebastianraschka.com/p/finetuning-l
arge-language-models
…). 

Small plot twist via 
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2202.10054

author: rasbt
text: I think the concern with mode collapse in Pre-LN comes from the fact that normalizing the inputs sort of squishes them. So somewhat different input may look more similar after normalization, which means that this could lead to a lower capacity to distinguish between diff inputs.

author: rasbt
text: I guess the exploding/vanishing gradient in Post-LN is probably due to the fact that the inputs to the layers are not normalized (think about training a deep network without Layer/BatchNorm). I mean, they are normalized in Post-LN but only after the residual connection.

author: rasbt
text:
But of course, the gold standard for evaluating LLMs is to ask human raters. Based on human evaluation, Self-Instruct outperforms base LLM, and LLMs trained on human instruction datasets in supervised fashion (SuperNI, T0 Trainer).

4/7

author: rasbt
text:
In practice, this works relatively well based on the ROUGE scores. For example, a Self-Instruct-finetuned LLM outperforms the GPT-3 base LLM (1) and can compete with an LLM pretrained on a large human-written instruction set (2).

3/7

author: rasbt
text:
Self-Instruct is one (almost annotation-free) way to align pretrained LLMs with instructions as illustrated in the figure above.

2/7

author: rasbt
text:
Instruction finetuning is how we get from GPT-3-like pretrained base models to more capable LLMs like ChatGPT. This requires human-generated instruction data like databricks-dolly-15k.
So, how do we scale this? One way is bootstrapping an LLM off its own generations.

1/7

The open source Pythia suite of LLM models is an interesting alternative to other decoder-style (aka GPT-like) models.

author: rasbt
text:
Last month was particularly exciting for open-source AI, featuring several open source implementations of large language models (LLMs).

Now we are seeing a big wave of open-source datasets! We had Databricks Dolly 15k and OpenAssistant Conversations for instruction finetuning.

Phew, maybe these:

1) run the model multiple times to make sure you have made sure you configured it correctly (in terms of determinism)

2) Use torch.float16 instead of torch.float32 with mixed precision

3) Use torch.compile when you run it on a single GPU

any suggestions?

author: rasbt
text:
My favorite deep learning tips for the start of the new week: 

1) Rewinding to the best epoch with model checkpointing
2) Debugging by overfitting to a minibatch
3) Looking at model architecture summaries
4) Using learning rate schedulers

1/2
